title: Family & Home
focus: Family members, describe people, rooms and where I live.
function: Describe the people I live with and where I live
foundationalLiteracy: Possessive 's in print. Compound words for rooms (bed-room, bath-room) as a decoding pattern.
culturalNote: This unit can be painful. Many newcomers are separated from parents, live with distant relatives or a sponsor, are unaccompanied minors, or have lost family members. Never assign a family tree as required work, never ask who a student lives with in front of peers, and always offer an alternative: describe a person who is important to you. Also avoid assuming a nuclear family — extended and multi-household families are the norm for most of the world.

1.canDo: Point to a family member in a picture when the word is said.
1.scaffold: Generic family picture cards, not the student's own family.
1.check: Identifies 6 family words from spoken input.

2.canDo: Understand a short spoken description of a person and pick them out.
2.scaffold: Four photos varying by one or two features. Description replayed on request.
2.check: Selects the correct person from 4 photos in 4 of 5 trials.

3.canDo: Follow a spoken description of a family and map the relationships.
3.scaffold: Blank relationship web to fill in while listening. Played twice.
3.check: Correctly places 5 people and their relationships from connected speech.

1.canDo: Name family members and say who I live with in single words.
1.scaffold: Picture cards. One-word answers fully accepted.
1.check: Names 4 family members and indicates who they live with.

2.canDo: Describe a person using two or three sentences.
2.scaffold: Frame: This is my ___. He/She is ___. Adjective bank with pictures.
2.check: Produces 3 framed sentences describing one person.

3.canDo: Describe my home and the people in it, and ask a partner about theirs.
3.scaffold: Open frame, adjective and room bank, one modeled question to ask back.
3.check: Describes home and people in connected speech and asks a reciprocal question.

1.scaffold: Word–picture cards with a model line already matched.
1.check: Matches 6 of 8 family words to pictures.

2.canDo: Read a short description of a family and answer who questions.
2.scaffold: 5-sentence passage with a picture and glossed vocabulary.
2.check: Answers 4 of 5 literal who and how many questions.

3.canDo: Read a longer description and infer a relationship that isn't stated directly.
3.scaffold: Passage plus an inference prompt: How do you know? Evidence sentence starter.
3.check: Makes one correct inference and cites the sentence that supports it.

1.canDo: Label pictures of people and rooms.
1.scaffold: Picture page with a word bank and labeling lines.

2.canDo: Write 3–4 sentences describing a person who is important to me.
2.scaffold: Frames plus an adjective bank. Student chooses the person freely.
2.check: Writes 3 sentences with a subject, a verb, and one adjective each.

3.canDo: Write a paragraph describing my home and who is in it.
3.scaffold: Paragraph organizer (topic sentence, 3 details, closing). There is/are reminder.
3.check: Writes a 5-sentence paragraph with a topic sentence and correct there is/are.

1.scaffold: Two picture cards — one person, one adjective. Student places them side by side and names them. Teacher expands into a sentence and the student echoes.

2.say: This is my [relation]. She is [adjective] and [adjective]. She lives in [place].
2.scaffold: Three-sentence frame with an adjective bank. Student may use a photo or a drawing.

3.say: The person I want to tell you about is my [relation]. She's [adjective], and she [action] every day. I miss her because [reason]. Who is important to you?
3.scaffold: Open frame with a because clause and a reciprocal question. The because is the academic-language move — this is where opinion becomes explanation.
